caveats: a: The wearable-classification study evaluated two horses and a riding use case, not commercial farm labor displacement.
a: Assisted reproduction expands genetic reach but still requires veterinary timing, mare management, and physical procedures.
rho: APHIS's 2026 national study is evidence of active measurement needs, not proof of AI implementation or labor savings.
rho: High-value breeding operations may adopt earlier than mixed, working-equine, or lower-value farms.
e: The 372,810 inventory farms are not a count of NAICS 112920 firms and must not replace frozen n.
e: Horse and pony sales can be incidental to a farm classified in another industry, while semen and stud revenue need not coincide with animal sales.
s5: TOTAL measures acres and transfer intentions, not completed operating-company control transactions.
s5: A dispersal or bloodstock auction may transfer productive assets without transferring the firm.
q: Keeneland's 2025 annual gross of $835.8 million across 5,955 sold horses is a premium thoroughbred-auction benchmark, not a national producer margin measure.
q: AQHA registrations and ownership transfers indicate market activity, not prices, profitability, or AI value sharing.
d5: Census sales are nominal and may reflect price, mix, or reporting changes rather than unit-demand growth.
d5: The broad horse economy includes excluded boarding, training, racing operations, recreation, events, and services.
o: The estimate concerns the need for an operating enterprise, not the number of employees or degree of automation.
o: Changes in total demand belong in d5 and should not be double counted as software displacement.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Sales through private treaty, agents, breed and discipline networks, online listings, and auctions, supplemented by stud fees, semen, and related breeding products; prices are highly differentiated by pedigree, conformation, health, age, training, performance, reputation, and buyer segment.
lens.excluded_activities: Boarding and training establishments, racing or spectator-sport owners, recreational owners, rescues and sanctuaries, veterinary practices, hobby or lifestyle farms, passive landowners, captive equine operations without material external production revenue, shells, non-control financing vehicles, and firms outside the roughly $1-10M normalized EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market independent commercial establishments primarily breeding and raising horses, ponies, mules, donkeys, or other equines for repeated sales of foals, breeding stock, performance or working animals, semen, stud services, or other equine products.

a.bridge: Equine research and veterinary sources demonstrate machine-learning gait classification, connected health monitoring, and assisted reproduction, but do not measure the wage-weighted share of not-yet-automated tasks at LMM breeding and raising firms. I map those capabilities, plus pedigree and breeding analysis, foaling alerts, pricing, marketing, scheduling, records, and office work, to exposure while discounting because feeding, mucking, handling, turnout, breeding, foaling intervention, transport, maintenance, and emergency care remain physical and context-sensitive. Research prototypes can overstate commercial readiness and sources focused on riding or veterinary reproduction can misstate breeder workflows.
caveats: The wearable-classification study evaluated two horses and a riding use case, not commercial farm labor displacement.
Assisted reproduction expands genetic reach but still requires veterinary timing, mare management, and physical procedures.
a.change_evidence: A task- and wage-weighted study of commercial equine breeders, separating already-automated work from new AI capabilities and reporting time spent on animal care, reproduction, training, facilities, sales, and administration, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: AI can improve observation, prediction, mating and reproductive decisions, documentation, and commercial administration. It is less able to substitute for direct animal care and accountable judgment where welfare, safety, and individual temperament matter, so the central exposure is below one-third of wage-weighted work.

d5.bridge: The Census reports national equine commodity sales and the American Horse Council estimates broad horse-industry scale, while Keeneland observes one premium auction channel; none is a constant-quality five-year demand forecast for NAICS 112920 breeders. I treat 2022 horse and pony sales of about $2.240 billion plus mule, burro, and donkey sales of about $12.6 million, versus $1.500 billion for the combined commodity group in 2017, as evidence of nominal demand resilience, then discount for inflation, animal mix, racing and recreation cycles, and Census comparability. Broad economic-impact figures can overstate breeder demand because they include care, competition, recreation, racing, and services outside the lens.
caveats: Census sales are nominal and may reflect price, mix, or reporting changes rather than unit-demand growth.
The broad horse economy includes excluded boarding, training, racing operations, recreation, events, and services.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year forecast of foals, registrations, imports and exports, commercial horse and breeding-product transactions, real prices, buyer participation, and retirements by breed and use would materially change the ratio.
d5.rationale: Demand is supported by recreation, competition, racing, ranch work, and breeding, and differentiated animals can command rising nominal prices. It remains discretionary and cyclical for many buyers, exposed to household wealth, feed and land costs, welfare regulation, breed preferences, and the long replacement cycle, supporting modest central growth with a wide range.

caveats: The 372,810 inventory farms are not a count of NAICS 112920 firms and must not replace frozen n.
Horse and pony sales can be incidental to a farm classified in another industry, while semen and stud revenue need not coincide with animal sales.
e.change_evidence: A business-level file combining primary NAICS, equine sales and stud revenue, boarding and racing revenue, ownership, normalized EBITDA, related entities, and operator status would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: The Census recorded horses or ponies on 372,810 farms in 2022 but only 63,018 farms reported horse or pony sales, illustrating substantial non-selling ownership even before excluding boarding, racing-only, recreation, rescues, captive use, marginal operations, and firms outside the EBITDA band. A six-digit NAICS assignment raises the likelihood of production activity relative to all horse-owning farms, but the frozen n is missing and no source resolves which establishments are independent control-ready LMM breeders.

l.detail: SUSB scope excludes this activity, no IPS labor-compensation/output series at any level, and no other receipts source downloaded; labor_share left null (recorded gap).
l.rationale: dataset labor_share has no defensible value

n.detail: margin 0.0804 (margins.json[11]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Farming/Agriculture EBITDA/Sales 8.04%); revenue/employee $299,458 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 11); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: The estimate concerns the need for an operating enterprise, not the number of employees or degree of automation.
Changes in total demand belong in d5 and should not be double counted as software displacement.
o.change_evidence: Evidence that integrated breeding platforms, contract farms, or reproductive-service providers assume animal ownership, welfare liability, and production responsibility at scale would materially change the operator-required share.
o.rationale: Almost all year-five demand for newly produced equines and breeding products still requires an accountable operator to select and manage breeding stock, care for animals, supervise reproduction and foaling, maintain land and facilities, document health and pedigree, sell animals, and bear welfare and commercial risk. AI, veterinarians, breeding services, and monitoring platforms can change staffing and boundaries but cannot independently own animals or discharge continuous husbandry obligations.

q.bridge: Keeneland and AQHA document active auction, registration, and ownership-transfer markets, but do not measure the five-year share of AI benefits retained by LMM equine producers. I translate pedigree, performance, health, reputation, scarcity, buyer trust, and relationship-specific breeding decisions into relatively strong retention, while allowing auction fees, agents, veterinarians, platforms, insurers, landowners, trainers, and sophisticated buyers to absorb gains. Keeneland's thoroughbred market materially overrepresents the premium end of the wider NAICS industry.
caveats: Keeneland's 2025 annual gross of $835.8 million across 5,955 sold horses is a premium thoroughbred-auction benchmark, not a national producer margin measure.
AQHA registrations and ownership transfers indicate market activity, not prices, profitability, or AI value sharing.
q.change_evidence: Farm-level margin bridges after analytics or monitoring adoption, repeat-contract and stud-fee data, auction and private-sale net proceeds, agent and veterinary fees, and changes in land, insurance, and software costs would materially change retention.
q.rationale: Equines are differentiated assets rather than fungible livestock: breeder reputation, bloodlines, conformation, health, training, and documented performance can sustain large price dispersion. Better selection, health, and marketing can therefore remain with credible producers, although transparent auctions, buyer bargaining, service-provider costs, and rapid diffusion reduce full capture.

caveats: APHIS's 2026 national study is evidence of active measurement needs, not proof of AI implementation or labor savings.
High-value breeding operations may adopt earlier than mixed, working-equine, or lower-value farms.
rho.change_evidence: Five-year commercial-farm cohorts reporting installed cost, adoption, false positives, interventions, labor hours, veterinary outcomes, breeding efficiency, downtime, insurance constraints, and payback would materially narrow the range.
rho.rationale: Records, marketing, customer communications, scheduling, pedigree analysis, cameras, wearables, and alerting can be implemented within five years, and assisted reproductive techniques are already operational. Realized substitution is constrained by fragmented data, breed-specific rules, sensor fit and durability, false alarms, rural connectivity, veterinary integration, liability, animal-welfare standards, small cohorts, and the continued need for humans to interpret and act on alerts.

s5.bridge: USDA reports an average producer age of 58.1 and measures expected farmland ownership transfers across all agriculture, not five-year qualifying control transfers of LMM equine-production businesses. I use roughly 5% of farmland slated for sale or gift as a low anchor and raise the firm-level center because operating control, bloodstock, brands, customer relationships, and leases may transfer without all land changing hands. The proxy can overstate actionable deals when land passes within families or trusts and understate transitions structured through stock, animal, or lease transfers.
caveats: TOTAL measures acres and transfer intentions, not completed operating-company control transactions.
A dispersal or bloodstock auction may transfer productive assets without transferring the firm.
s5.change_evidence: Equine-specific ownership-age, succession-plan, dispersal, brokerage, and closed control-transaction data for commercial breeders in the target earnings band would materially change the probability.
s5.rationale: Family ownership and aging operators support succession activity, but equine businesses are unusually tied to owner reputation, animal portfolios, facilities, land, and breed networks. Internal succession, dispersal sales, piecemeal bloodstock sales, and property leases can replace an intact third-party control transaction.

principal_driver: Strong differentiation through bloodlines, performance, health, and breeder reputation can protect value created by better monitoring, breeding decisions, documentation, and commercial execution.
principal_weakness: Missing labor and firm-count denominators, combined with pervasive hobby, boarding, racing, and incidental horse ownership, prevent a reliable estimate of actionable LMM acquisition capacity.

caveats: S2's exact official PDF was reachable, but its detailed equine counts and sales values could not be independently text-verified through the audit tooling.

S10 was blocked by a 403 response, leaving its registration and ownership-transfer counts unverified; this weakens q and d5 support but cannot change the record's EVIDENCE_FIRST action while l and n remain MISSING.

The a and rho source set establishes a two-horse gait-classification prototype, established reproductive techniques, and a forthcoming national measurement study, not the broader pedigree, pricing, administrative, monitoring, adoption, or labor-substitution claims. The record honestly labels these inputs PROXY and ESTIMATE and discloses the mismatch.

The q base is a disclosed judgment inferred from product differentiation and market activity rather than measured AI-benefit retention, and the d5 base is inferred partly from nominal sales values and broad-industry activity rather than constant-quality quantity demand.

The s5 bridge extrapolates from all-agriculture operator demographics and acreage-transfer intentions to intact equine-business control transfers; family succession, asset dispersals, and land-only transactions may make the firm-transfer rate materially different even though this is explicitly disclosed.

sources_audited.note: The Census NAICS page defines 112920 as establishments primarily raising horses and other equines and explicitly separates equine boarding and racing or spectator-sport owners.

The exact official Census of Agriculture PDF returned HTTP 200, but its large PDF body could not be text-extracted through the audit viewer, so the detailed equine counts and sales values were not independently confirmed in-page.
The report-form guide distinguishes owned horses sold, nonowned or boarded horses, and equine breeding fees, supporting the stated measurement boundary.

The paper describes machine-learning gait classification from smartwatch accelerometer data with smartphone or cloud processing and evaluation on two horses; the packet accurately treats it as a narrow technology proxy.
UC Davis describes artificial insemination using fresh, cooled, or frozen semen, its genetic-reach benefit, and the need for careful ovulation timing and mare management.

APHIS supports the stated scope of the 2026 national equine study and its all-state randomized farm phase, but it is evidence of measurement activity, not connected-health implementation, AI adoption, or labor savings.
USDA reports that family-owned and operated farms were 95% of U.S. farms and that average producer age was 58.1; the packet discloses the agricultural rather than equine-specific population.

USDA reports about 43 million acres, roughly 5% of U.S. farmland, slated for ownership transfer within five years and 23 million acres expected to sell to nonrelatives; the packet expressly treats acreage intentions as a broad proxy.

The Keeneland page's embedded annual table reports 2025 totals of $835,820,200 and 5,955 sold, 2024 totals of $682,204,700 and 6,079 sold, and 2023 totals of $659,672,100 and 6,336 sold, matching the packet's rounded figures.
The exact AQHA Facts URL returned a Cloudflare 403, so the claimed 2024 registration and ownership-transfer counts could not be reopened or verified.

The American Horse Council page reports 6.65 million U.S. horses and $177 billion of economic impact and presents a broad horse-industry scope; the packet appropriately warns that this is not breeder demand alone.

sources.supports: Official scope covering establishments primarily raising horses, mules, donkeys, and other equines, while classifying boarding in 115210 and racing or spectator-sport owners in 711219.
sources.title: NAICS Sector 11 Definitions: 112920 Horses and Other Equine Production

sources.supports: National equine evidence: 372,810 farms held 2,411,232 horses or ponies; 63,018 farms sold 267,890 for $2.240 billion; 98,726 farms held 257,825 mules, burros, or donkeys and 8,474 farms sold 28,181 for $12.627 million.
sources.title: 2022 Census of Agriculture, United States Summary and State Data

sources.supports: Measurement boundary distinguishing owned horses sold, nonowned or boarded horses, and horse breeding or stud fees, semen, and other equine products.
sources.title: 2022 Census of Agriculture Report Form Guide

sources.supports: Technology proxy showing machine-learning classification of horse gaits using smartwatch accelerometer data and smartphone or cloud processing, evaluated on two horses.
sources.title: A framework for the recognition of horse gaits through wearable devices

sources.supports: Operational reproduction context: artificial insemination uses fresh, cooled, or frozen semen to expand stallion genetics but requires careful ovulation timing and mare management.
sources.title: Equine Assisted Reproductive Techniques

sources.supports: Current operating-context evidence: the 2026 national study covers equine care, health management, disease, veterinary services, and at-risk or vocational transition across randomly selected operations in all 50 states.
sources.title: USDA's National Animal Health Monitoring System Launches National Equine Study

sources.supports: Farm-structure proxy: 95% of US farms were family-owned and operated, the average producer age was 58.1, and 74% had sales of $50,000 or less.
sources.title: USDA releases 2022 Census of Agriculture data

sources.supports: Transfer proxy: about 43 million acres, roughly 5% of US farmland, are slated for sale or gift within five years, including 23 million acres expected to sell to nonrelatives.
sources.title: Most of the U.S. Rented Farmland is Owned by Non-Farmers

sources.supports: Premium auction proxy: 2025 Keeneland live sales totaled $835.820 million across 5,955 sold horses, compared with $682.205 million across 6,079 in 2024 and $659.672 million across 6,336 in 2023.
sources.title: Keeneland Annual Sales Figures

sources.supports: Breed-market proxy: AQHA reported 90,986 registrations and 105,982 horse ownership transfers in 2024.
sources.title: AQHA Facts

sources.supports: Broad demand proxy estimating 6.65 million US horses and $177 billion of total economic impact, while separately identifying racing, recreation, competition, traditional work, assisted services, and sanctuaries.
sources.title: 2023 Economic Impact Study of the Horse Industry

title: Horses and Other Equine Production
